original_abstract: Selective modification of nitrogen heteroaromatics is a cornerstone of drug discovery and molecular imaging, yet methods for the direct exchange of core skeletal atoms—atom transmutation—remain sparse. This paper describes a mild and efficient protocol for the $^{14}$N to $^{15}$N isotopic exchange of various nitrogen-containing heterocycles, including pyrimidines and pyridines. The transformation leverages a skeletal editing approach where the heteroaromatic substrate is activated via $N$-triflylation, followed by a room-temperature Zincke-type ring-opening/ring-closure sequence mediated by an $^{15}$N-labeled aspartate nucleophile. A key feature of this process is the formation of a stable $N$-succinyl intermediate, which undergoes base-mediated elimination to release the $^{15}$N-labeled heterocycle in high chemical and isotopic yields. The substrate scope encompasses a diverse array of functionalized and bioactive molecules, and a modified workup procedure allows for the isolation of products with isotopic purity matching the enrichment of the starting reagent. Additionally, DFT-based computational modeling and decision-tree analysis identify key stereoelectronic descriptors, such as the $N–C_6$ bond distance, that reliably predict the efficiency of isotopic incorporation across different heterocyclic frameworks. This methodology provides a practical tool for the rapid synthesis of labeled probes for NMR spectroscopy, metabolomics, and spin hyperpolarization studies.
humanized_abstract: Atom transmutation—selective core skeletal modification—constitutes an important aspect of small-molecule drug development and molecular imaging. Herein, we show that a range of nitrogen-containing heteroaromatics, including both bioactive pyrimidines and pyridines, can be successfully subjected to $^{14}$N to $^{15}$N atom transmutation in a quantitative, isotropic fashion under mild conditions. Our skeletal editing approach initially involves $N$-triflylation to enhance the reactivity of the aromatic ring, followed by a zwitterionic room temperature Zincke-type ring-opening/ring-closing reaction with an $^{15}$N-enriched aspartate. This reaction affords an $N$-succinyl derivative that is stoichiometrically converted to the $^{15}$N-labeled heterocycle upon base catalysed neutralization. Our protocol is shown to be applicable to a wide variety of both functionalised and bioactive heteroaromatics. An optimised downstream work-up is also demonstrated to deliver product of enhanced $^{15}$N-isotopic purity equal to that of the $^{15}$N aspartate reactant. Using a combination of density functional theory and decision tree analysis we ascertain a subset of stereo-electronic variables that serve to reliably assess the potential for $^{14}$N to $^{15}$N exchange across different hetero-cyclic systems. Our $^{14}$N to $^{15}$N isotopic exchange protocol also affords products that are amenable for further application in $^{15}$N NMR spectroscopy, metabolomics and spin hyperpolarisation experiments.
